note_data.title: Sound
keyPoints: **Sound** is produced by vibrating objects which create compressions and rarefactions in the air
Sound requires a **medium** to travel through - it cannot travel through a vacuum
Sound travels as **longitudinal waves** where particles vibrate parallel to the direction of wave travel
Speed of sound in air at 20°C is approximately **340 m/s**
Speed of sound is faster in **solids** than liquids, and faster in liquids than gases
**Echo** is the reflection of sound waves from a hard surface
The **audible range** for humans is approximately 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz
**Pitch** depends on frequency - higher frequency produces higher pitch
**Loudness** depends on amplitude - greater amplitude produces louder sound

definitions.definition: Longitudinal waves produced by vibrating objects that travel through a medium
The reflection of sound waves from a hard surface
How high or low a sound is, determined by the frequency of the sound wave
The maximum displacement of particles from their rest position in a sound wave
A substance through which sound waves can travel, such as air, water or solids

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or steps in logical order
State similarities and differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain why sound cannot travel through a vacuum but can travel through air. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Sound waves are vibrations that require particles to transmit energy from one place to another. In air, particles vibrate and pass energy to neighbouring particles, allowing sound to travel. A vacuum contains no particles, so there are no particles to vibrate and transmit the sound energy.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Sound requires particles/medium to travel
Mark 2: Air contains particles that can vibrate
Mark 3: Vacuum has no particles to transmit vibrations

commonMistakes: Never say 'sound travels faster in gases' - sound travels fastest in solids, then liquids, then gases
Students often confuse pitch with loudness - pitch depends on frequency, loudness depends on amplitude
Don't say 'sound is a transverse wave' - sound is always a longitudinal wave
Never write 'sound travels at the speed of light' - sound travels much slower than light
Students forget that echoes require hard surfaces for reflection - soft surfaces absorb sound
examinerTips: Echo questions appear frequently - remember distance = speed × time, but sound travels to surface and back
Always state that sound requires a medium when explaining why it cannot travel through space
Use the word 'vibrations' when describing how sound is produced
Temperature affects speed of sound - sound travels faster in warmer air
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Longitudinal wave diagram showing compressions (particles close together) and rarefactions (particles spread apart) with particles vibrating parallel to wave direction
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (3 marks) — they asked students to arrange gas, liquid, and solid in order of sound speed from slowest to fastest
N/D 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — required identification of ultrasound frequency range from given options
M/J 2024 P2 Q6 (4 marks) — calculation of sound speed using v = d/t with discussion of experimental errors
N/D 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — comprehensive question on measuring sound speed including sources of inaccuracy in timing measurements

workedExample.question: A student measures the speed of sound by clapping wooden blocks 172 m from a large wall. The time between the clap and hearing the echo is 1.0 s. Calculate the speed of sound. Explain why this value is only approximate. (5 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that sound travels to wall and back, so total distance = 2 × 172 m = 344 m
Step 2: Apply formula v = d/t where d = 344 m and t = 1.0 s
Step 4: Explain approximation due to reaction time errors when starting/stopping timing
Step 5: Additional errors include difficulty measuring exact distance and temperature effects on sound speed
workedExample.answer: Speed = 344 m/s. Value is approximate due to human reaction time errors in timing, difficulty measuring distance precisely, and temperature variations affecting actual sound speed.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for doubling distance, 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for calculation, 2 marks for explaining sources of error

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates forgot to double the distance in echo calculations' — always remember sound travels to the reflector AND back when calculating from echo time
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates confused infrasound and ultrasound frequency ranges' — ultrasound is ABOVE 20,000 Hz, infrasound is BELOW 20 Hz
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students incorrectly stated that sound travels fastest in gases' — remember sound speed increases with density: fastest in solids, slowest in gases
note_data.keyDiagram: Echo method diagram showing: person with sound source, measured distance 'd' to wall/cliff, sound wave path going to reflector and returning (labeled as total distance 2d), stopwatch showing time measurement, with clear labels for 'incident sound', 'reflected sound', and the equation v = 2d/t prominently displayed.
note_data.quickRecap: Sound speed increases from gas to liquid to solid due to particle arrangement. Use v = d/t but remember to double distance for echo methods. Ultrasound is above 20,000 Hz, infrasound below 20 Hz. Main experimental errors are reaction time and distance measurement inaccuracies.
